definitions.definition: A person who studies past human cultures through the recovery, analysis, and interpretation of material remains such as artifacts, architecture, and cultural landscapes.
The scientific process of uncovering and analyzing artifacts from the past, typically through the systematic removal of soil and other materials from a site.
An object or remains of an ancient culture that are of interest to archaeologists, such as tools, pottery, or other items.
The remains or traces of plants, animals, or other organisms from the remote past, preserved in the Earth's crust.
A framework used by geologists to divide Earth's history into distinct periods of time, based on the types of rocks and fossils found in different layers.
The process of preserving a dead body through natural or artificial means, often involving the removal of internal organs and the use of chemicals to prevent decay.
The Egyptian pharaoh who ruled from approximately 1332 to 1323 BCE, known for his well-preserved tomb and the treasures found within.
The ruler of ancient Egypt, often considered a god-king who wielded absolute power and authority.
A large burial ground, often used by ancient cultures to honor their dead and provide a place for them to rest.
A stone or wood container used to encase a body for burial, often decorated with intricate carvings or other forms of art.
A structure used to bury or entomb the dead, often containing artifacts and other treasures meant to accompany the deceased on their journey.
A system of writing used by ancient Egyptians to record their language and stories, often featuring complex symbols and images.
A dead body that has been preserved through natural or artificial means, often used to study the culture and practices of ancient civilizations.
A type of paper made from the pith of the papyrus plant, often used by ancient Egyptians for writing and other purposes.
A symbol of rebirth and regeneration in ancient Egyptian culture, often depicted as a beetle or other insect.
A mythical creature with the body of a lion and the head of a human, often associated with ancient Egyptian culture and mythology.
The well-preserved tomb of the Egyptian pharaoh Tutankhamun, discovered by Howard Carter in 1922 and considered one of the most significant archaeological finds of the 20th century.
A vast burial ground in Egypt where many pharaohs and other high-ranking officials were entombed, often featuring elaborate tombs and treasures.
The ancient Egyptian god of mummification and the afterlife, often depicted as a jackal or other canine animal.
The ancient Egyptian god of the afterlife, often depicted as a man with green skin and associated with the cycles of nature and rebirth.
The ancient Egyptian goddess of magic, fertility, and protection, often depicted as a woman with a throne on her head and associated with the pharaohs and their power.
The ancient Egyptian concept of balance and order, often depicted as a woman with an ostrich feather on her head and associated with the pharaohs' role in maintaining justice and morality.
